_provenance: Claude-authored original passages (DeepSeek not used). Kaoyan 七选五/选句填空 difficulty: academic/abstract argumentative prose, longer sentences and sophisticated discourse markers. Each passage (250-340 word tokens, gaps removed) has 5 removed whole sentences (gaps 1-5), 7 full-sentence options (5 correct + 2 distractors). answers[i] = bank index for gap (i+1).

sets.passage: The phrase "paying attention" is more than a metaphor; in a digital economy, human attention has quietly become a genuine currency that platforms compete fiercely to extract. (1)______ Because the human capacity for focus is strictly finite while the supply of content is effectively infinite, the true scarcity in our age has shifted from information itself to the cognitive bandwidth required to process it. Advertisers, consequently, no longer purchase space so much as they purchase discrete moments of human concentration, and they are willing to pay handsomely for them. (2)______ Every notification that interrupts a meal, every video that begins playing of its own accord, and every feed that refuses to end is, on closer inspection, a carefully engineered bid for that scarce resource. This arrangement carries subtle and underappreciated consequences for the individual user. (3)______ When the design that proves most profitable also happens to be the design that distracts most effectively, the interests of the user and the interests of the platform quietly but decisively diverge. Critics of the industry argue that the resulting environment steadily erodes our capacity for the sustained, voluntary concentration on which serious thought depends. (4)______ Defenders of the platforms counter that users freely choose to participate and remain at liberty to disengage at any moment they wish. Yet this comfortable defence badly underestimates how deliberately such systems are calibrated to bypass our deliberation altogether. (5)______ Learning to regard attention as a contested economic resource, rather than as a purely private mental habit, may well be the first step toward reclaiming genuine control over how we choose to spend it.
bank: The notion rests on a simple premise with surprisingly far-reaching implications.
The mechanics of this competition are visible everywhere once one knows where to look.
What ordinary people experience as a personal failure of willpower is frequently the predictable product of a profitable design.
Long-term studies of habitual users suggest that fragmented attention gradually hardens into the default mode of thought.
The much-celebrated freedom to leave means very little when the real cost of staying has been carefully hidden from view.
Most advertisers still prefer roadside billboards because their costs are so easy to calculate in advance.
Reading a printed morning newspaper was once the principal way in which ordinary families learned about the wider world.
sets.explain_zh: (1) 上句说注意力成为货币，需引出其背后前提，选 0("该观念建立在一个影响深远的前提上")，下句"Because…"正是展开此前提。(2) 上句讲广告主购买专注时刻，选 1("这种竞争的机制随处可见")，下句以通知/自动播放具体化。(3) 转向对个人的影响，选 2("普通人体验为意志力失败的，常是可获利设计的可预测产物")，与下句"利益悄然分歧"衔接。(4) 批评者说侵蚀持续专注，选 3(长期研究显示碎片化成默认思维模式)。(5) 反驳"可随时离开"的辩护，选 4("离开的自由意义不大，留下的代价被隐藏")。干扰项 5(广告主偏好广告牌因成本易算)与"购买注意力"逻辑相反；6(印刷报纸)偏离注意力经济主题，放任何空都不连贯。

sets.passage: Despite repeated and confident predictions that modern technology would eventually scatter populations harmlessly across the open countryside, the world's great cities have continued, decade after decade, to swell. (1)______ The puzzle only deepens when we pause to recall that urban life is frequently more expensive, more crowded, and more stressful than the rural alternatives it has displaced. Economists who have devoted careers to studying this apparent paradox tend to point toward a single powerful force, which they call agglomeration. (2)______ When large numbers of talented and ambitious people choose to cluster together in one place, ideas circulate far faster, highly specialised firms can readily find the particular workers they require, and chance encounters on the street spark collaborations that no central planner could ever have arranged in advance. The economic benefits, in other words, are not merely additive but genuinely compounding in nature. (3)______ A larger city, on this account, does not simply happen to contain more talented individuals; it actively makes each unit of that talent more productive than it would ever be in isolation. This insight helps to explain a striking and well-documented statistical regularity across the globe. (4)______ Such consistent patterns strongly suggest that sheer scale itself, rather than any one particular industry or accident of geography, drives much of a city's enduring economic vitality. None of this, however, means that relentless urban growth is somehow costless. (5)______ Yet so long as the gains from proximity continue to outweigh these steadily mounting burdens, the powerful magnetic pull of the great city is unlikely to weaken any time soon.
bank: This stubborn and recurring fact has quietly surprised more than a few professional forecasters.
The term itself describes the productive advantages that arise simply from people being physically close to one another.
Researchers have repeatedly found that doubling a city's population tends to raise its economic output per person by a measurable margin.
Congestion, pollution, and ever-soaring rents impose very real and tangible costs on all those who crowd in.
Many small and isolated towns owe their entire survival to the presence of a single large employer.
Exactly the same multiplying logic explains why the most remote villages so reliably attract heavy investment.
It is precisely this multiplying effect that distinguishes living cities from mere lifeless collections of buildings.
sets.explain_zh: (1) 上句说技术本应分散人口却城市仍膨胀，选 0("这一顽固反复出现的事实令预测者意外")，下句"The puzzle deepens"承接。(2) 上句提出 agglomeration 一词，选 1("该词描述人们物理上靠近所产生的生产优势")作定义。(3) 上句说收益是复合而非相加的，选 6("正是这种倍增效应使活的城市区别于死的建筑堆砌")。(4) 上句"a striking regularity"需具体数据，选 2(城市人口翻倍人均产出上升)。(5) 上句否定"growth is costless"，选 3(拥堵/污染/高租金的代价)，与下句"so long as gains outweigh these burdens"呼应。干扰项 4(小镇靠单一大雇主生存)与"规模驱动活力"论点相左；5(偏远村庄可靠吸引投资)与 agglomeration 逻辑相反，放任何空都不连贯。

sets.passage: Common sense holds that having more options always makes us better off, on the comfortable assumption that a wider menu can only ever expand our chances of finding what we truly want. (1)______ Beyond a certain critical point, each additional choice we are offered begins to subtract from our overall satisfaction rather than reliably add to it. Psychologists who study human decision-making have carefully documented this counterintuitive reversal in settings as varied as crowded supermarkets and complicated retirement plans. (2)______ When confronted with twenty-four competing varieties of gourmet jam laid out on a table, ordinary shoppers proved far less likely to buy any jar at all than when they were offered a modest selection of only six. The explanation lies partly in the sheer mental cost of comparison itself. (3)______ Each fresh option must be carefully weighed against every one of the others, and the cumulative effort of doing so can become genuinely and surprisingly exhausting. Beyond this practical burden, there is also a subtler emotional toll that abundance quietly imposes on us. (4)______ When we finally do commit to a decision, the great abundance of rejected possibilities continues to haunt us, and we cannot help wondering whether some unchosen path might have served us rather better. Regret, in precisely this sense, tends to grow in proportion to the size of the menu before us. (5)______ The real lesson here is emphatically not that freedom of choice is undesirable, but rather that the careful architecture of our choices deserves quite as much attention as their mere number.
bank: A steadily growing body of careful evidence, however, complicates this comfortable and familiar assumption.
One particularly celebrated experiment involved an inviting supermarket display of gourmet jams.
As the available alternatives multiply, the cognitive work of evaluating each of them rises sharply.
More options, paradoxically, simply mean more vivid opportunities to imagine everything we have given up.
Curiously, satisfaction and choice can begin to move in opposite directions once a certain threshold has been crossed.
Most ordinary consumers happily buy the very first product they happen to encounter on any shelf.
Retirement plans are, on the whole, far too dull a subject to provoke any genuine emotional response at all.
sets.explain_zh: (1) 上句陈述"选项越多越好"的常识，选 0("然而越来越多的证据使这一假设变复杂")转折，下句"Beyond a certain point"承接。(2) 上句说在超市/养老金等场景观察到逆转，选 1("一项著名实验是果酱展示")，下句"twenty-four varieties of jam"承接。(3) 上句讲比较的心理成本，选 2("备选越多评估工作急剧上升")。(4) 上句引出情感代价，选 3("更多选项意味更多想象放弃了什么")，与下句"rejected possibilities haunt us"呼应。(5) 上句说后悔随菜单变大，选 4("越过阈值后满意与选择反向变动")收束。干扰项 5(消费者买第一个看到的产品)与"评估成本"矛盾；6(养老金太枯燥不引发情绪)与文中养老金例子及情感主题相悖，放任何空都不连贯。

sets.passage: Gossip has long suffered from a thoroughly poor and largely undeserved reputation, routinely dismissed by polite society as idle or even actively malicious talk that wastes precious time and needlessly wounds the reputations of others. (1)______ Anthropologists who study how human communities actually manage to hold themselves together tend to tell a considerably more complicated and interesting story. They argue persuasively that talking about absent others is in fact one of the oldest and most remarkably efficient ways our species has ever devised of transmitting vital social information. (2)______ Long before the invention of written records, formal courts, and impersonal institutions, a person's social standing within a group depended almost entirely on what their immediate neighbours quietly said about them. Seen in this revealing light, ordinary gossip functions rather like an informal but powerful system of accountability. (3)______ The simple knowledge that one's own behaviour will inevitably be discussed by others encourages people to honour their commitments faithfully, even when no formal authority is anywhere watching. The very same practice also works to strengthen the personal bonds between those who choose to share it with one another. (4)______ To be entrusted with genuinely sensitive information is, in effect, to be quietly treated as a valued insider, and exchanging such confidences in private steadily reaffirms a relationship over time. Of course, exactly the same social mechanism can readily be turned to cruel and destructive ends. (5)______ But to condemn the entire practice outright is to overlook completely the quiet and indispensable work it performs in sustaining the cooperative life of any human group.
bank: Yet this casual dismissal entirely overlooks the genuine social purposes that such talk has long quietly served.
It allows a tightly knit group to learn very quickly who among them can be relied upon and who cannot.
Reputation, after all, is enforced largely through precisely what people choose to say behind closed doors.
Sharing a private confidence signals a depth of mutual intimacy that ordinary small talk never possibly could.
Vicious false rumours can swiftly destroy a hard-won reputation that may have taken many years to build.
Total silence, on the whole, is surely the most reliable sign of a genuinely healthy and trusting community.
Most ancient societies in fact kept astonishingly detailed written records of every single commercial transaction.
sets.explain_zh: (1) 上句贬低八卦，选 0("然而这种轻视忽略了此类谈话长期默默服务的社会目的")，下句"more complicated story"承接。(2) 上句说八卦是传递社会信息最古老高效的方式，选 1("它让紧密群体迅速知道谁可靠")。(3) 上句称八卦是非正式问责系统，选 2("声誉很大程度靠人们背后所言来维系")，与下句"行为会被议论促使守信"呼应。(4) 上句说强化共享者纽带，选 3("分享私密信息表明亲密")。(5) 上句"can be turned to cruel ends"，选 4(恶意谣言可摧毁多年声誉)。干扰项 5(沉默是健康社区标志)与全文"八卦有益"矛盾；6(古代社会有详细书面记录)与"书面记录之前"相悖，放任何空都不连贯。

sets.passage: No complex society could possibly function for long if each of its members had to verify every single promise made to them before acting upon it. (1)______ Trust, in this respect, is the invisible social lubricant that allows complete strangers to transact, cooperate, and rely confidently upon one another without the need for constant and costly supervision. Economists have long observed that high-trust societies tend, on average, to be substantially wealthier, in part because they spend so much less of their resources on the elaborate machinery of enforcement. (2)______ Written contracts can be shorter and simpler, everyday transactions can proceed faster, and bitter disputes become markedly rarer whenever people generally expect others to keep their given word. Yet trust, for all its evident value, remains a curiously fragile thing, far easier to destroy outright than ever to rebuild from scratch. (3)______ A single well-timed betrayal can permanently poison a relationship that may have taken many patient years of reliable conduct to establish in the first place. This deep asymmetry helps to explain precisely why personal and commercial reputations are so jealously guarded by those who hold them. (4)______ Each commitment that is faithfully honoured is, in economic effect, a small but real investment in one's own future ability to cooperate with others. Importantly, however, genuine trust should never be confused with mere naivety or wishful thinking. (5)______ The most resilient and durable forms of human cooperation rest not on blind faith at all but on a well-founded and reasonable confidence that others will in fact reciprocate. Wherever that confidence manages to take firm root, the hidden costs of social life steadily fall while its many rewards quietly multiply.
bank: Such an exhausting requirement would render ordinary daily life impossibly slow, awkward, and expensive for everyone.
The resulting savings are by no means merely financial but extend significantly to time and mental energy as well.
Confidence that has been built up slowly through countless small interactions can nonetheless collapse in a single instant.
A solid reputation for reliability is surely among the most valuable assets that any person can ever hope to hold.
It is rather a reasonable and calculated bet, firmly grounded in past behaviour and widely shared expectations.
Most commercial contracts today are drafted by expensive lawyers who openly distrust one another entirely.
Genuinely wealthy societies generally go out of their way to discourage their citizens from cooperating at all.
sets.explain_zh: (1) 上句说若须核验每个承诺社会无法运转，选 0("这种要求会使日常生活慢得不可能且昂贵")，下句"Trust is the invisible lubricant"承接。(2) 上句说高信任社会更富裕、执法成本低，选 1("节省不仅是财务还延伸到时间精力")。(3) 上句说信任脆弱、易毁难建，选 2("经无数互动缓慢建立的信任可瞬间崩塌")，与下句"single betrayal"呼应。(4) 上句讲声誉被珍视，选 3("可靠声誉是最宝贵资产之一")，与下句"investment in future cooperation"呼应。(5) 上句"trust is not naivety"，选 4("它是基于过去行为的合理押注")，与下句"not blind faith but well-founded confidence"呼应。干扰项 5(合同由互不信任的律师写)与"高信任省成本"矛盾；6(富裕社会阻止公民合作)与全文相悖，放任何空都不连贯。

sets.passage: Modern life offers an ever-expanding array of clever conveniences, each one cheerfully promising to save us a little time and spare us a little effort along the way. (1)______ The deeper and far more interesting question is what exactly we quietly surrender in exchange for all that hard-won ease. Consider, for a moment, the everyday human skills that convenience steadily and silently renders unnecessary. When a small device confidently navigates the city for us, our own innate sense of direction slowly atrophies; when an application faithfully remembers all our appointments, our unaided memory is exercised far less than before. (2)______ Mental and physical capacities that consistently go unused tend, over a long enough stretch of time, to weaken and eventually fade. There is also a quiet social cost involved that rarely if ever appears printed on any receipt. (3)______ Services that conveniently let us avoid all real interaction with shopkeepers, neighbours, and passing strangers may indeed spare us some minor friction, but they simultaneously thin out the dense web of casual human contact that silently knits a genuine community together. Convenience, moreover, very often works to shift its hidden costs quietly elsewhere. (4)______ The cheap and instantly disposable product that saves a busy household a precious few minutes may, on closer inspection, impose a far larger and more lasting burden on the natural environment or on distant and invisible workers. None of this, to be perfectly clear, argues for rejecting modern convenience wholesale. (5)______ But a far clearer and more honest accounting of what each tempting shortcut truly costs us would surely help us to choose more wisely which human efforts are genuinely worth keeping.
bank: We almost always tend to greet such shiny new innovations with a kind of uncritical, reflexive enthusiasm.
The human brain, after all, faithfully follows a rather stern and unforgiving principle of use it or lose it.
Unchecked convenience can quietly erode the small daily encounters on which durable social ties ultimately depend.
What feels almost completely effortless to us as individuals is very frequently not effortless for the wider world.
Hardly any of us would willingly choose to return to a harsh life entirely without running water or refrigeration.
Most modern conveniences, when studied carefully, turn out to have no measurable effect whatsoever on how we actually live.
Strangers, as a sensible general rule, are very much best avoided altogether in any large and busy city.
sets.explain_zh: (1) 上句说现代生活提供大量便利，选 0("我们几乎总是不加批判地欢迎这些新创新")，下句"deeper question"转折。(2) 上句说方向感/记忆因便利退化，选 1("大脑遵循'用进废退'原则")，与下句"unused capacities weaken"呼应。(3) 上句引出社会成本，选 2("不受约束的便利会侵蚀社交纽带所依赖的日常小接触")，下句"avoid interaction"承接。(4) 上句说便利常把隐藏成本转移别处，选 3("对我们个人毫不费力的，对更广世界往往并非如此")，下句"impose burden on environment/workers"呼应。(5) 上句否定"全盘拒绝便利"，选 4("少有人愿回到没自来水的生活")，与下句"But a clearer accounting"呼应。干扰项 5(便利对生活无可测影响)与全文"有代价"矛盾；6(陌生人最好回避)与"日常接触维系社区"相悖，放任何空都不连贯。

sets.passage: There is a now-widespread cultural worry that we are slowly losing our collective capacity for deep reading, that is, the slow and wholly immersive engagement with long, demanding, and intricately argued texts. (1)______ The genuine concern here is emphatically not that people read less in sheer total, for in plain fact we now read more words every single day than any previous generation ever did, but rather that the very kind of reading we typically do has fundamentally changed. Rapid skimming, restless scanning, and constant jumping between disconnected fragments now thoroughly dominate most of our everyday encounters with the written word. (2)______ Such ingrained habits are admirably well suited to processing a relentless daily flood of short messages and headlines, yet they sit deeply uneasily with the patient, undivided attention that any genuinely complex argument inevitably requires of us. Neuroscientists studying the question now strongly suggest that the human brain physically adapts itself to whatever particular mode of reading we happen to practise the most. (3)______ The neural circuits painstakingly trained for rapid surface skimming may well grow steadily stronger over time, but often at the direct expense of those that quietly support slow and reflective comprehension. All of this genuinely matters because deep reading is by no means merely a pleasant private pastime for the leisured few. (4)______ Patiently following a sustained argument to its end, holding several competing ideas in mind at once, and fully entering into another person's unfamiliar perspective are cognitive and even moral exercises of very real and lasting value. The worry, then, is finally not a sentimental or nostalgic one but an eminently practical concern. (5)______ Actively preserving the necessary conditions for deep reading may, in the end, demand the very same deliberate effort that we willingly devote to protecting any cherished skill we genuinely fear losing.
bank: This particular anxiety has grown noticeably louder as bright glowing screens have come to mediate so much of what we now read.
Online, the restless human eye is relentlessly trained to dart toward whatever happens to be the brightest and the briefest.
Reading, to put the striking point another way, physically reshapes over time the very organ that actually performs it.
It carefully cultivates particular mental capacities that other, shallower forms of reading rarely if ever manage to touch.
If any human faculty can be genuinely strengthened through steady practice, then it can surely also fade away through long neglect.
Old-fashioned printed books are, of course, almost always far more expensive to buy than convenient digital subscriptions.
People who habitually skim a very great deal usually end up understanding genuinely difficult texts far more thoroughly than others.
sets.explain_zh: (1) 上句提出担忧深度阅读能力流失，选 0("随着屏幕中介了大量阅读，这种焦虑更响")，下句"not that people read less"承接。(2) 上句说浏览/跳读主导，选 1("网上眼睛被训练扑向最亮最短的东西")。(3) 上句说大脑适应常练的阅读模式，选 2("阅读重塑执行它的器官")，下句"circuits…at the expense of"呼应。(4) 上句说深读不只是消遣，选 3("它培养其他浅阅读少触及的能力")，下句具体列举。(5) 上句"not nostalgic but practical"，选 4("能力可经练习增强也会因荒废消退")，与下句"deliberate effort"呼应。干扰项 5(纸书比数字订阅贵)偏离能力主题；6(常浏览者更透彻理解难文)与全文论点矛盾，放任何空都不连贯。

sets.passage: Few intellectual exercises are quite as reliably humbling as carefully reviewing past predictions about the future of technology, a field in which even the most confident expert forecasts so consistently miss the mark. (1)______ The underlying reasons for this remarkably persistent record of failure are genuinely instructive rather than merely amusing to us. One recurring difficulty is that professional forecasters tend, almost irresistibly, to extrapolate from the familiar present, imagining simply more of what already comfortably exists rather than something genuinely and disruptively new. (2)______ They confidently pictured faster and stronger horses, in effect, at the very moment when the real future quietly held automobiles. A second and quite distinct problem concerns the eventual social uses of any technology, which are notoriously difficult to anticipate correctly in advance. (3)______ The humble telephone, for one famous instance, was first earnestly marketed as a serious tool for broadcasting live concerts into the home, and almost no one foresaw the intimate personal conversations that would later come to define it. There is also the stubborn matter of timing, which routinely defeats even those rare forecasters who happen to guess the eventual destination quite correctly. (4)______ A genuine technical breakthrough may turn out to be entirely possible decades before the surrounding infrastructure, the falling cost, or the wider culture finally allows it to spread widely. Perhaps the very deepest lesson buried in all of this is ultimately one of intellectual humility. (5)______ Honestly recognising the sharp limits of our own foresight does not for a moment excuse us from the hard work of planning ahead, but it certainly should teach us to hold all our confident forecasts rather more loosely.
bank: Recorded history is positively littered with confident assertions that eventually aged into a kind of unintentional comedy.
Truly disruptive inventions, almost by their very definition, lie stubbornly outside the comfortable patterns we already know.
How real people actually choose to adopt a new invention very frequently ends up surprising even its own creators.
Simply knowing for certain that something eventually will happen tells us remarkably little about precisely when it will happen.
The future, it seems, has a stubborn habit of arriving in concrete ways that no recognised expert quite managed to imagine.
Most serious technological forecasts, when examined closely, turn out to have been remarkably accurate in hindsight.
Live concerts broadcast directly by telephone remain, even today, the single dominant form of mass entertainment worldwide.
sets.explain_zh: (1) 上句说回顾技术预测令人谦卑、常落空，选 0("历史满是变成无意喜剧的断言")，下句"reasons for this failure"承接。(2) 上句说预测者从当下外推，选 1("真正颠覆性发明本就在已知模式之外")，下句"faster horses"承接。(3) 上句说技术的社会用途难料，选 2("人们实际如何采用常令发明者意外")，下句电话例子呼应。(4) 上句讲时机问题，选 3("知道会发生并不告诉我们何时发生")，下句"breakthrough possible decades before infrastructure"呼应。(5) 上句"deepest lesson is humility"，选 4("未来以无专家想象的方式到来")，与下句"limits of foresight"呼应。干扰项 5(预测事后看相当准)与全文"常落空"矛盾；6(电话广播音乐会至今是主流娱乐)与事实及文意相悖，放任何空都不连贯。

sets.passage: When we sit down to think about the relationships that have genuinely shaped the course of our lives, we naturally and immediately picture our close friends and family, the strong ties bound tightly together by deep affection and frequent daily contact. (1)______ Yet sociologists studying human networks have gradually uncovered a genuinely surprising truth about precisely the looser and more casual connections that we are otherwise inclined to overlook entirely. These so-called weak ties, the various acquaintances whom we happen to see only occasionally, frequently prove rather more useful to us than our very closest companions in certain crucial and surprising respects. (2)______ The real reason for this lies squarely in the underlying structure of our social networks rather than in the emotional warmth of any single human bond. Our closest friends, after all, almost invariably tend to move within the very same social circles that we ourselves already inhabit day to day. (3)______ They typically read the same news, already know most of the same people, and naturally hear about the same handful of opportunities, so the information they can offer us largely just duplicates whatever we already happen to possess. A genuinely distant acquaintance, by sharp contrast, very often inhabits an almost entirely different social world from our own. (4)______ Through precisely these outward links to people unlike ourselves, genuinely novel information about jobs, fresh ideas, and unexpected possibilities steadily flows into our lives from distant regions we could not otherwise possibly reach. This single sociological insight quietly carries a rather important practical implication for all of us. (5)______ Once we picture each casual acquaintance as exactly such a span linking two separate worlds, patiently nurturing a wide and genuinely varied circle of them may, in the long run, matter quite as much as faithfully deepening a small handful of intimate lifelong friendships.
bank: We very rarely think to credit our casual acquaintances with much real importance in the larger affairs of our lives.
One famous and much-cited study found that most ordinary people actually land their new jobs through exactly such distant contacts.
Their available knowledge therefore tends to overlap very heavily with whatever store of information we already comfortably possess.
Precisely because they are not at all close to us, such people reliably connect us outward to entirely fresh circles of others.
The humble weak tie thus quietly acts as an invaluable bridge spanning two otherwise completely separate social worlds.
Our close friends are, frankly, generally quite useless to us whenever we happen to be searching hard for any new opportunity.
Mere acquaintances, quite unlike genuine friends, can simply never be trusted with anything that truly matters to us.
sets.explain_zh: (1) 上句说我们自然想到强联系，选 0("我们很少认为泛泛之交在生活大事中重要")，下句"Yet…surprising truth about looser connections"转折承接。(2) 上句说弱联系在某些方面更有用，选 1("著名研究发现多数人靠这种远距联系找新工作")。(3) 上句说密友与我们同圈，选 2("他们的知识因此与我们已有的高度重叠")，下句"duplicates our own"呼应。(4) 上句说远交住在不同世界，选 3("正因不亲近，他们把我们向外连到全新圈子")，与下句"outward links to people unlike ourselves"(向外连接、与己不同)唯一呼应；index 4"桥梁"已无"bridges"词面诱导，不再适配此空。(5) 上句"practical implication"，下句"such a span linking two separate worlds"(分隔两个世界的跨接)前指，唯一选 4("弱联系因而充当分隔世界间的宝贵桥梁")；index 3"向外连到新圈子"不含"跨接两个分隔世界"故不适配，gap4/gap5 不可互换。干扰项 5(密友在找机会时毫无用处)过于绝对且与"as much as deepening friendships"矛盾；6(熟人永不可托付要事)与文意无关且绝对，放任何空都不连贯。

sets.passage: Imagine, for the sake of argument, a wide green pasture left entirely open to all the herders of a small village, where each one of them is perfectly free to graze upon it as many of his own animals as he personally likes. (1)______ Every single additional animal that is added yields a concentrated private gain directly to its individual owner, while the gradual resulting overgrazing of the land is quietly shared out among the whole community alike. Because the benefit is so sharply concentrated while the real cost is so widely diffused, each individual herder rationally finds it perfectly sensible to add yet another animal to his growing herd. (2)______ The shared pasture is ultimately ruined precisely and only because every single individual involved has behaved entirely sensibly from his own narrow point of view. This relentless underlying logic, now widely known as the tragedy of the commons, in fact reaches very far beyond mere village grazing land. (3)______ Great ocean fisheries collapse without warning, ancient underground aquifers slowly run completely dry, and the shared atmosphere steadily fills with harmful emissions, all through precisely the same remorseless underlying arithmetic. What makes this whole stubborn problem so genuinely difficult is that simple moral exhortation, all on its own, very rarely works in practice. (4)______ Appealing earnestly to private conscience merely disadvantages the genuinely responsible while quietly rewarding the selfish, since voluntary restraint by any one user simply leaves that much more for all of the rest to grab. Lasting and effective solutions therefore tend, in the end, to involve deliberately changing the basic rules of the whole game itself. (5)______ Whether through carefully shared ownership, strictly enforceable quotas, or thoughtfully assigned individual rights, the central aim throughout is to align each separate individual's private incentives with the broader welfare of the whole.
bank: At first glance, this comfortable old arrangement seems entirely harmless and even rather pleasant enough.
The inevitable collective result of all these perfectly reasonable individual decisions is, paradoxically, collective ruin.
It accurately describes virtually any situation in which a genuinely shared resource is exploited steadily until it is exhausted.
Simply telling people earnestly to use rather less, all by itself, ends up cruelly punishing only the conscientious among them.
Most successful human communities solve the underlying dilemma only by deliberately altering the very incentives that first created it.
Village herders, as absolutely everyone already knows perfectly well, naturally cooperate freely without any need for formal rules.
Private walled gardens are surely the single clearest example of a resource that simply no one could ever possibly overuse.
sets.explain_zh: (1) 上句描绘公共牧场设定，选 0("乍看这安排无害甚至宜人")，下句"Every additional animal yields private gain"承接揭示问题。(2) 上句说每个牧人都理性增畜，选 1("所有这些合理个体决定的集体结果是集体毁灭")，下句"ruined precisely because everyone behaves sensibly"呼应。(3) 上句说此逻辑超越牧场，选 2("它描述任何共享资源被耗尽的情形")，下句渔业/含水层例子呼应。(4) 上句说劝诫无用，选 3("叫人少用只惩罚有良知者")，与下句"disadvantages the responsible"呼应。(5) 上句说要改变游戏规则，选 4("多数社区只有改变制造困境的激励才能解决")，下句"shared ownership/quotas/rights"承接。干扰项 5(牧人天然合作无需规则)与全文核心矛盾；6(私家花园无人能过度使用)与"共享资源"主题不符，放任何空都不连贯。
